conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To properly assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

First, let me review the official eligibility rules so I can guide you accurately.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, **do you have any children?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have one child, a son named Alex. He's 8 years old. I'm hoping to find out if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great that you have Alex, who is 8 years old. Since he's under 16, that meets the age requirement.

Now I need to ask: **Do you live with Alex in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK – I'm in Bristol, actually. But Alex doesn't live with me. He lives with his nan at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Alex lives with his nan rather than with you.

**Are you currently contributing financially towards Alex's upkeep?** For example, are you providing money for things like food, clothes, pocket money, or contributing towards his accommodation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out with Alex. I buy him clothes, give him pocket money, help with school dinners – that sort of thing. I try to contribute what I can regularly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. To determine if your contributions are sufficient, I need to understand the amount.

**Are you contributing at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment would be for Alex?** (For reference, Child Benefit for one child is currently around £25 per week, or roughly £100-£108 per month.) 

Do your total contributions for Alex's upkeep amount to at least this much?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'd say so. I spend about £30 a week on him, so that should be more than enough to cover it.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. That's very helpful.

Just a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex** (such as his nan or another family member)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him at the moment. His nan isn't getting it for him or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

One final question: **Is the local council paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance while he's living with his nan?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, there's nothing like that. It's just an informal arrangement between me and his nan – the council isn't involved at all.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! Based on the information you've provided, you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

✓ **Alex is 8 years old** - under the age limit of 16
✓ **You live in the UK** (Bristol)
✓ **You're considered responsible for Alex** - even though he doesn't live with you, you're contributing approximately £30 per week towards his upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount (around £25 per week)
✓ **No one else is claiming Child Benefit** for Alex
✓ **No local council involvement** in his care or maintenance

Since you're regularly contributing at least as much as the Child Benefit payment towards Alex's food, clothes, pocket money, and school dinners, you meet the criteria for being responsible for him.

You can go ahead and apply for Child Benefit for Alex. Just be aware that if you or your partner earn over £60,000 individually, you may need to pay a High Income Child Benefit tax charge, though you can still claim the benefit.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which is under 16, so he meets the age requirement. Although Alex does not live with the claimant, the claimant is contributing approximately £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep (including clothes, pocket money, and school dinners). This amount exceeds the Child Benefit payment amount (approximately £25 per week for one child). According to the eligibility rules, you'll usually be responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards looking after them. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. The claimant lives in the UK (Bristol). Therefore, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (aged 8), who lives with his nan. The claimant lives in the UK and contributes approximately £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and there is no local council involvement. The claimant meets the eligibility criteria as they are responsible for Alex by contributing at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his care.

Therefore, the claimant qualifies for Child Benefit for Alex.

The claimant lives in the UK and contributes approximately £30 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and there is no local council involvement. The claimant meets the eligibility criteria as they are responsible for Alex by contributing at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards his care.
